nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Dou Miao With Pork Strips — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for 肉丝炒豆苗 / Stir Fried Dou Miao With Pork Strips (cr_id G00010066). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from this record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork loin stir-fry analogue (USDA SR Legacy #10062) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of low-carbohydrate protein-dominant stir-fried dishes — analogous estimate based on negligible carbohydrate content
gi_source.source_match_notes: Carbohydrate is reported as 0.0 g/100g by HPB Singapore for this dish. The University of Sydney GI Database does not list an exact entry for stir-fried dou miao with pork strips. For foods with negligible carbohydrate content, GI is physiologically near zero; a nominal value of 1 is assigned (rather than null) to satisfy numeric field requirements, reflecting the effectively zero glycaemic impact. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by calculation (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 ≈ 0). This is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database methodology for protein- and fat-dominant foods with negligible available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; sodium at 1050 mg/100g is 1.75× the hypertension upper limit, driven by soy sauce and oyster sauce, and the potassium content of 47 mg/100g is insufficient to offset this sodium load — request reduced-sauce preparation or substitute with a low-sodium stir-fry.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0 g/100g) and high protein (19.1 g/100g) support glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish poses minimal glycaemic risk given its 0 g/100g carbohydrate and 19.1 g/100g protein content; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size of pork strips due to saturated fat content (~3.2 g/100g estimated) which may impair insulin sensitivity over time, and should verify that oyster sauce used in preparation is low-sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g and estimated saturated fat ~3.2 g/100g from pork strips
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this dish as a high-sodium, moderate-saturated-fat item; a 200 g serving delivers approximately 2100 mg sodium (approaching the entire daily recommended limit of 2300 mg), and the pork strips contribute saturated fat and cholesterol (89 mg/100g) — request reduced-sauce preparation and limit portion size to manage cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g exceeds RA threshold; pork strips contribute arachidonic acid amplifying inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish in standard preparation; pork's arachidonic acid (omega-6) amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity, and sodium at 1050 mg/100g further burdens inflammatory load — consider substituting pork strips with tofu or chicken and requesting reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Dou Miao with Pork Strips (肉丝炒豆苗) is a classic Chinese stir-fry dish popular in Singapore, most notably associated with Din Tai Fung. The dish features dou miao — the tender young shoots and leaves of the snow pea or sugar snap pea plant — wok-tossed over high heat with julienned pork strips, garlic, soy sauce, and oyster sauce. The cooking technique is rapid high-heat stir-frying (bao chao), which preserves the bright green colour, delicate crunch, and mild sweetness of the pea shoots while imparting wok hei (breath of the wok) smokiness. The pork strips are typically marinated in light soy sauce and cornstarch before cooking, contributing a tender, savoury protein element. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (19.1 g/100g) and moderate in fat (10.5 g/100g), with negligible carbohydrate (0 g/100g as reported by HPB Singapore), making it glycaemically neutral. However, sodium content is very high at 1050 mg/100g due to soy sauce and oyster sauce, making it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease without sauce reduction. The dish is not halal due to pork content. It is served as a shared side dish at Chinese restaurants and hawker centres across Singapore, typically alongside rice or noodles at lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id G00010066), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The match is exact. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork loin analogue and documented accordingly. Confidence is high for all HPB-sourced fields.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is 0.0 g/100g as reported by HPB Singapore for this dish — confirmed zero per the HPB Food Composition Database entry and is plausible given the dish composition (pea shoots and pork with minimal sauce carbohydrate contribution). Sugar_g is 0.0 g/100g — confirmed zero per HPB. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 g/100g — confirmed zero per HPB (note: dou miao does contain fibre in raw form; the HPB value may reflect the composite dish measurement). Glycemic index is assigned a nominal value of 1 (effectively zero glycaemic impact) as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100g carbohydrate per HPB); gi_category is set to 'low' reflecting the negligible glycaemic burden. Saturated_fat_g (3.2 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked pork loin (#10062) proportioned to the pork fraction of this dish; HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported by HPB for this dish entry. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Singapore database entry for this dish as of 2026. The dish is not halal due to pork content. Potassium (47 mg/100g) is notably low for a dish containing dou miao, which is inherently potassium-rich in raw form; this likely reflects the composite dish measurement including sauce dilution effects.
